FACTS
The facts are substantiallY the
Mr. Wellington.
CONCLUS IONS
1. The public generally exception applicable to smal1
jurisdictions does not appl-y to the Marina city council because
ihe city's population exceeds the number required to.fit 
wlthin
the excLption in Regulation 18703.1. The city council's
', jurisdiation,tt as defined in the Act, includes the population in
the annexed portions of Fort ord.
2. other agencies may have jurisdictions that are larger or
snal-ler than the city boundaries. If the planning commission's
jurisdiction expressl-y excludes the Fort ord area, that population

The city of Marina is situated west of the United States Army
base at Fort ord. Several years ago, Marina annexed a portion of
Ford ord, adding 13,380 persons to the city's existing population
of ]-6,733.
The city of Marina does not provide nunicipal services to the
area, such as police or fire protection, and does not coLlect
property taxes from the area. In addition, the area is not
iufJect- to the cj-ty's transient occupancy tax, property transfer
taxl planning and land use/building inspection fees and the sales
and'uie tax on retaif sales. However, the city does receive tax
benefits based on population due to the inclusion of the area.
Further, the residLnts of that area do vote in cj'ty elections'
Because the city covers a srnall geographic area, you have
been having problerns with multiple disqual if ications on both the
city councit and planning commission Ievels, due to conf Li'cts of
intLrest. 
You have asked whether the public Atenerally exception
applicable to small jurisdictions, 
Regulatj'on 18703.1, applj-es to
tirl city council or ilanning commission of Marina where the city
boundar-ies include 30,113 persons, but the city's population
independent of the federal areas is only l'5,733.
Additional information was obtained by letter dated
october :-9, Lggo, which was subnitted by Assistant City Attorney
Kenneth D. Buchert, and fron a meeting wittr l'tr. Buchert and Marina
Planning Director Jeffrey P. Dack- We have incorporated this
information in grr analYsis.


Our Fi■e No. I-90-535a
Page 3
ANALYSTS
1. conflicts of Interest GeneraIlv
The Act prohibits any public official 
from making,
participating in rnaking, or otherwise using his official 
position
to influence a governmentaL decision in hrhich the official 
has a
financial interest. 
(section 871o0.) Section 87103 provides that
an official 
has a f i.nancial interest in a decision within the
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable . that the
decision will have a rnaterial financial- effect, distinquishable
fron the effect on the public generallv, on the official 
or a
member of his or her innediate farnily or on::
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1'0oo) or nore.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dol]ars ($1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than Loans by a commercial lendingr
institution in the regular course of business on
terms avaj-Iab1e to the Publ-ic without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or rnore in va1-ue provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the tine $rhen the
decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of, a gj-ft or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or prornised to the pubJ.ic
off i,cia1 wj.thin 12 nonths prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public o(jicial, 
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, 
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, 
the official's 
agents,
spouse, and dependent children own directly,
indirectly, or beneficial-Iy a 1O-percent interest
or greater.


Our File No. I-90-535a
Page 4
For example, under the confl ict-of- interest provisions of the
Aet, officials with interests of 51,000 or more in real property
may not participate in any governnental decision which will have a
reasonablv foreseeable material financial effect on the official's
real property.
2. The Public Generallv ExceDtion
However, pubJ-ic officials with financial j-nterests that wilI
be materialLy affected by a decision may participate in the
decj-sion if the effect on their interest is not distinguishable
fron the effect on the public generall,y. (Regulation 18703, copy
enclosed. ) The public generally exception applies only if the
decision will affect the official's 
interests in substantiallv the
sane manner as it would affect a sionificant secnnent of the public
qenera I lv .
The ttpublicl consists of those persons within the
jurisdiction of the respective officials, 
i.e., the residents and
persons doing business in the jurisdiction of the agency i'n
(uestion. 
(In re owen (L976) 2 FPPC ops. 77, copy enclosed. ) In
fn re Leqan ( (1985) 9 FPPC ops. 1, copy enclosed) the Commission
concluded that perSOns could constitute part of the public even
respect to those oersons:
The t'pub]ic" includes the entire jurisdiction
for the agency in question, particularly where an
elective body is being considered, because all of
the County's residents are constituents of the
various members of the Board of Supervisors and
many reside in the various incorporated cities
wittrin the county. They are all inpacted in one
sray or another by the county's land-use decisions,
evLn if not directly subject to the County's land-
use jurisdiction. 
We see no reason to alter our
inteipretation that the residents, and businesses
and pioperty owrrers of the county of santa clara
are the- "puLl-ic" for purposes of decisions being
made by the Board of SuPervisors.
fn re Leqan, suPra, at 12.
Thus, under the traditional public aenerally exception,
populations in federal areas nust also be considered a part of the
;Gii; 
tenerally, despi-te limited power on the part of the city
touncil-to direl-tlv control the area' Hence the exception only
;;;ii;; 
if the d-ecision wilr affect the official's 
interests in
sulstantial.Iv the sarne rnanner as it would affect a siqnificant
@ 
popu).ation of Marina, including Fort ord'


Our File No. I-90-535a
Page 5
3. The Marina citv council and Requlation 18703.1
Regulation 18703.1 (copy enclosed) provides an additional
public generally exception for small jurisdictions. 
Pursuant to
iegulation l-8703.1 the effect of a governmental decision on a
public official's 
principal residence is indistinguishable from
the effect on the public generally if all the requirenents of the
regulation are net. Subdivisi.on (a) of Regulation 18703.1
provides:
(a) The effect of a governmental decision on
the principal residence of a public official- is not
distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally v/here all- of the following conditions are
met:
(1) The public official's 
agency has
jurisdiction over a population of 25,000 or
1ess, covering a geographic area of ten square
miles or Iess.
You have asked for reconsideration of our concl-usion that
under subsection (a) (r.) of Regulation L8703.1 the city of Marina
would be considered to have jurisdiction over the entire
population of the city, incfuding persons residing at Fort ord.
A. The Geoqraphic component of Jurisdiction
Section 82035 provides:
"Jurisdictionrr means the state with respect to
a state agency and, with respect to a Ioca1
government agency, the region, county, city,
district gI other qeographical area in which it has
jurisdiction. 
The jurisdiction of a menber of a
regional coastal zone conservation conmission shall-
be the permit area in lrhich the regional commission
has jurisdiction. 
ReaI property shall be deemed to
be trwi-thin the jurisdiction" with respect to a
local government agency if the property or any part
of it is located within or not more than two rniles
outside the boundaries of the jurisdiction or
within two rniles of any land owned or used by the
Ioca1 governnent agency.
IEmphasis added. ]
め


Our File No. I-90-535a
Page 6
Thus, the Act sets forth a geographic definition of
rrjurisdiciion[ for state and ]-ocal government agencies- - (In re
B;tv (1979) 5 FPPC ops. 10, copy enclosed. ) r/ 
For example, the
luri=diction of a state agency is the. state, apparently
lrrespective of federal enclaves lrithin. 
(Stevenson Advice
letter, No. A-81-02-010, copy enclosed.)
For loca1 government agencies, the jurisdiction-is 
the
geographic area covered by the county, city or district' 
Thus,
ior-dilclosure purposes we have said that a candidate for city
council rnust diiclose all his reat property interests that are
within the city limlts, and t$ro mi-les beyond. (Harris Advice
Letter, A-80-03-015, coPY enclosed. )
Moreover, it appears that the clear intent of Regulation
18703.1 was to consider the entire population e'ithin the
geograpnic boundaries of a city as within the jurisdiction-of 
the
6ii,-"'""""i1 
for purposes of subdivj.sion (a)'. As described.in our
initiar 
advice lette; to Mr- vlellington, the ranguage used in
rnernoranda submitted to the cornmission in the course of adopting
neluiation 18703.1 never suggested that a city council' could have
iuiisaiction 
over a population less than that encompassed-by the
6i[;;;-;";"daries. 
lcornmission Menorandum: Pre-notice Discussion
I-pi"p"i"a Regulation 1g703.1; Comrnission Memorandum: Adoption of
e.Lp"'""a neguiation 18703.1r copies enclosed.) The data contained
in those meioranda concerning the number of cities vrhich would be
affected by the Proposed regulation which was subrnitted by the
League ot bities- appears to be based on the cities' total
population.
By annexing the Fort ord area, the city of.Marina
affirmitivety aited to add the area and population.of Fort Ord to
the geographic jurisdiction of the city of Marina.'/
cons6qu6nt1y, Mirina's "jurisdictionrr for Purposes of the Act
includes the population in the annexed portions of Fort Ord'
3/ In discussing the appropriate interpretation of the definition
of rrjurisdictioni for nunicipal- court judges, the Commission
stat6d: 
rtHowever, the courts are not considered either local
governnent or state agencies as those terms are defined in the
ict. 
[citation omitt;d] 
While the jurisdiction of the courts is
not specifical,ty defined by section 82035, we bel'ieve that the
geogriphical corcept enunciated in that sectj-on should be applied
to the courts . tr 
( In re Batv, S.!lE!.a r at 11 . )
4/ "Annexationtt is defined in Black's Law Dictionary (1979) as
ttthe acquisition of territory or land by a nation, state or
rnunicipitity; the legal incorporation of a tot/n or city into
another town or citY. rl


Our File No. I-90-535a
Page 7
B. The Authoritv component of "Jurisdictionrl
we have also noted that the traditional definition of
"jurisdictionrr also incorporates an authority component. Funk and
wagnalls standard col-1ege Dictionary (1974) provides an
atternatj,ve definition of "jurj.sdiction" as the lal^rfu1 ttright to
exercise officiat 
authority, s/hether executive, legislative or
judicial.rl
In a Letter submitted by Cofonel craig S. Schlrender of the
Staff Judge Advocate/s office, colonel Schwender explains the
relationship bet$reen the city of Marina and the federal government
with respect to Fort Ord.
The Federal government bv statute has
perrnitted states to exercise certain powers within
an area of exclusive federal jurisdiction. 
An
example is the Buck Actr (4 U.S.c. Sec. 105-110)
here states are permitted to exercj-se certain
taxing powers Icitation onitted]. 
Another exampl-e
relates to the right of resi-dents in federal
enclaves to vote in state el-ections, where a
deprivation of that right would constitute a
violation of the Fourteenth Anendment of the United
states Constitution Icitation onitted].
It is important to understand that the consent
bv the Federal Government for a state to exercise
certain powers within an area of exclusive federal
iurisdiction does not constitute a retrocession of
the portion of that exclusive jurisdiction to the
state. 
The term " jurisdicti,onrr refers to the
authority to legislate. 
By giving its consent to
the exercj-se of certain state powers, the FederaL
Government has not surrendered its residual-
exclusive iurisdiction. 
It merelv permits the
state to exercise certain powers until the
permission is subseguentfv withdrawn Icitation
omitted I .
The annexation of a portion of an area of
excl-usive federaL legislative jurisdiction by a
loca1 municipality in no way operates in derogation
of these principals Icitation omitted].
lEmphasis added l
-


Our Fi■e No. 1-90-535a
Page 8
we do not quarrel with colonel Schwender's statement that the
federal government has not surrendered its residual exclus j-ve
jurisdiction to the city of Marina. Hor,rever, as stated in our
prior advice letter, while the city council's authority with
respect to the area in question is limited and apparently
contingent on federal consent, the city council, nevertheless,
continues to have sone authoritv and official 
duties with respect
to the region. We believe colonel Sch$render's letter supports
this conclusj,on. The power to tax the population of Fort ord
suggests at least some authority over those persons. Thus, r^/e
conclude that the city council maintaj-ns some jurisdictj-on over
the Fort Ord area notwithstanding the quality or quantity of that
jurisdiction.
c. Policv Considerations
In implernenting the Act the Commission has found that it is
necessary to look at the goals and intent of the Act as a $rhole,
rather than C10Se■
y scrutinizing the components of whatever
definition may be applicable in a given situation.5′   (In re witt
(1976) 1 FPPC Ops. 1, copy enclosed.)
There are strong pol-icy reasons not to chanqe our
interpretation of jurisdictj-on as set forth in the WeLlincrton
Advice Letter. 
It would appear that not only are the persons
residing in the federal area constituents of the city council, but
they may also be impacted by city council decisions. 
Since the
population in Fort ord presumably does business in Marina, almost
any city council decision which has an irnpact upon the population
of I'larina al,so affects the population of Fort ord. For example, a
decision concerning an increase in park fees would have an equal
inpact upon federal- personnel at Fort ord and city residents.
Consequently, as discussed in the guote from fn re Leqanr EllElg r
the popul,ation in Fort ord is affected in one way or another by
city decisions, just as are the other residents, even if it is not
subject to the city's land use jurisdiction.
In addition, persons residing in the federal areas have a
constitutional right to vote for city councilnembers and
presunably a right to representation by the city council. 
we
believe this factor further supports the conclusion that a city's
jurisdiction is the entj,re city, without the exclusion of any
segnents of the population.
5/
section 8■003 states that ::this tit■
e should be libera■
ly
construed to accompLish its purposes.tr In addition, in watson v.
california Fair Political Practices comnission (r99o) 2L7
CaI.App.3d 1059, the court affirmed the duty of the Commission to
implement the intent rather than the literal 
Language of statutes
that make up the Act.


Finally, as stated in our initial 
tetter, Regulation 18703-1
is an exception to the general rules requiring disgua 1i f ication on
the basis of an official's 
econornic interests. 
As such, a narrow
interpretation of this exception is preferred. (Julius Goldman's
Eqq citv v. Air Pollution controt Dept. of ventura countv (1981)
l-16 Ca1.App.3d 741.)
4. other Local Government Acrencies
However, the strj,ct geographic definition of "jurisdiction"
i-n the Act does not apply to every I'ocaf government agency. Not
aLl- loca1 governnent lgencies have a jurisdiction fixed by city
boundaries. For example, some local government agencies have been
set up with jurj,sdictions that are greater than the city in which
they inay exiit. 
For example, Joint Powers Authorities have
jurisailtions 
that are set up over a geographic area greater than
i single city. 
In our letter to Andre de Bortnoh,ski (Advice
Lettei, No. I-90-356; See also, Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454;
Dickerson Advice Letter, No. 76-256, copies enclosed) we
concluded:
Under your facts, the agency's redevelopnent
po$/ers are }imited by the Assenbly BiIl that
Lreated the aqency to the area within a 3-miLe
radius of Norton Air Force Base- Accordingly, the
area currently occupied by Norton Air Force Base
and all the area within a 3-mi1e radius of the base
are part of agency's jurlsdiction for disclosure
and disqua I i f ication purposes of the Act. In
addition, real property shall be deened to be
"within the jurisdi.ctionrt wi,th respect to a local
governnent agency if the property or any.part of it
is located within or not more than two miles
outside the boundaries of the jurisdiction or
within two rniles of any land owned or used by the
local government agency.
In other cj-rcumstances, 1ocal governrnent agencies may have
jurj,sdictions $/hich are small-er than a city in which the are
6ased. For example, the jurisdiction of the San Diego Unified
Port District was described as trover certain San Diego Bay tide
and submerged lands which are held in trust for the benefit of the
people of the state of California for the purposes of commerce,
navigation, fisheries and recreation" in our advice letter to
,foseffr D, iatel,lo (Advice Letter No. A-84-096, copy enclosed. ) 6/
consequentlJ, where the Marina Planning Conmission's
jurisdiction isahe city of Marina, the population in Fort ord is

within the jurisdiction of the planning comrnission.T/ Hosrever,

The Federal government bv statute has

Ford ord, adding 13,380 persons to the city's existing population
of ]-6,733.
The city of Marina does not provide nunicipal services to the
area, such as police or fire protection, and does not coLlect

iufJect- to the cj-ty's transient occupancy tax, property transfer

and'uie tax on retaif sales. However, the city does receive tax
benefits based on population due to the inclusion of the area.
